metadata.title: Install n8n and Meet the Workflow Builder
metadata.playlist: Shop for Ingredients — Collect Your Madison Data
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 3 brief — Shop for Ingredients: Collect Your Madison Data

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 7. n8n is a visual workflow builder — you drag boxes onto a canvas, connect them with lines, and click Execute. That is the whole mental model. You do not write code; you wire nodes. Today you get n8n running on your own machine, learn the five things on the canvas you need to know, and end with a blank workflow ready for your three sources. Two commands, a browser tab, and about fifteen minutes. Let's go.

props.segment: Install n8n and Meet the Workflow Builder
props.command: Get n8n running locally and understand the canvas before building anything.
props.runningText: two commands, a browser tab, about 15 minutes…
output: Nodes = steps; connections = data flow; Execute = run

beats.narration_text: Let's define the terms before we install anything. n8n is a visual workflow automation tool. A workflow is a sequence of steps — you describe what data to fetch, how to transform it, and where to save it, and n8n executes those steps in order. Each step is called a node. A node is one unit of work: fetch from an API, read a CSV file, filter rows, write to a file. You connect nodes by drawing a line between them — that line is the data path. When you click Execute, n8n runs the nodes in sequence, passes the output of each node to the input of the next, and shows you what came out at each step. This is different from a cloud service — n8n runs on your own computer, at localhost:5678. The data never leaves your machine unless you explicitly send it somewhere. For this assignment, everything stays local.

artifactLines: n8n: visual workflow automation tool — runs on your computer, not in the cloud.
Workflow: a sequence of steps that fetch, transform, and save data.
Node: one unit of work — fetch / filter / transform / write.
Connection: a line between nodes — the data path from one step to the next.
Execute: runs the nodes in order; shows output at each step.
localhost:5678: where your n8n runs — data stays local unless you send it out.
props.sparkLine: Nodes are steps. Connections are pipes.

beats.narration_text: Installing n8n requires Node.js on your machine. If you have not installed Node, go to nodejs.org and install the current LTS version first — the page will show you which one. Once Node is installed, open your terminal and run: npm install -g n8n. The -g flag installs n8n globally so you can run it from any directory. This will take a minute or two. When it finishes, run: n8n start. You should see output in the terminal ending with 'Editor is now accessible via: http://localhost:5678/' Open that URL in a browser. If you see the n8n canvas, you are done. n8n's documentation at n8n.io/docs covers version-specific differences — the UI changes across releases, so check the docs if any screen you see does not match what is described in this video. The course YouTube playlist also has walkthrough videos. Check both before assuming something is broken.

artifactLines: Prerequisite: Node.js installed — get the LTS version at nodejs.org if needed.
Step 1: npm install -g n8n   (installs globally; takes 1–2 minutes)
Step 2: n8n start            (starts the server)
Step 3: Open http://localhost:5678 in a browser.
If you see the canvas: done. If not: check n8n.io/docs or the course YouTube playlist.
Note: n8n's UI changes across versions — the docs are the source of truth for your version.
props.sparkLine: npm install -g n8n → n8n start → localhost:5678

beats.narration_text: When you open localhost:5678 you will see the workflow canvas. Five things to know. First: the node panel on the left — this is the library of everything n8n can do, organized by category. Search 'RSS' and you will find the RSS Read node. Search 'HTTP' for the HTTP Request node. Second: the canvas itself — the blank space where you drag and drop nodes. Third: to add a node, click the plus button in the canvas or drag from the node panel. Fourth: to connect two nodes, hover over the right edge of a node until you see a dot, then drag from that dot to the left edge of the next node. Fifth: the Execute button — top right. Click it to run the workflow. When a node runs successfully, it shows a green checkmark. When it fails, it shows a red indicator and an error message in the panel below. Read the error message before you assume the setup is wrong. Most errors name the field that is missing or misconfigured.

props.artifactHeading: Canvas tour — five things to know
artifactLines: 1. Node panel (left): search for nodes by name — 'RSS', 'HTTP', 'CSV', 'Write Binary'.
2. Canvas: blank space; drag nodes here; zoom with scroll or trackpad.
3. Add a node: click the + button or drag from the panel.
4. Connect nodes: drag from the right edge of one node to the left edge of the next.
5. Execute button (top right): runs the workflow; green = success, red = error + message.

beats.narration_text: Three traps that show up on first install. First: Node not installed. npm install -g n8n will fail with 'command not found' if Node is not on your machine. The fix is to go to nodejs.org and install the LTS version first — this is a one-time setup. Second: wrong port. n8n defaults to port 5678. If something else on your computer is already using that port, n8n will either use a different port or fail to start. Read the terminal output — it prints the URL it is actually running on. Third: expecting a cloud app. n8n is not Zapier. It does not have an account to log in to. It runs on your computer. When you close the terminal window, n8n stops. To use it again, open the terminal and run n8n start. Keep the terminal open while you are working.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Node not installed: npm fails with 'command not found' → install Node LTS first at nodejs.org.
Trap 2 — Wrong port: n8n prints the actual URL in the terminal — read it if localhost:5678 is blank.
Trap 3 — Expecting a cloud app: n8n runs locally; closing the terminal stops it; run 'n8n start' to restart.
Rule: keep the terminal window open the entire time you are building.
props.sparkLine: Read the terminal. It tells you what went wrong.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Install n8n. Two commands: npm install -g n8n, then n8n start. Open localhost:5678 in a browser — if you see the canvas, you are ready to build. A node is one unit of work. Connections are data paths between nodes. The Execute button runs the sequence. If something fails, read the error message before assuming a broken install — almost every first error names the missing field or misconfigured setting. n8n.io/docs is the authoritative reference for your version; the course YouTube playlist has additional walkthroughs. Before Episode 3, confirm you have your API key for whichever Tier-1 API you chose — YouTube Data API or NewsAPI. You will need it when you wire the API node.

props.artifactTitle: Install n8n and Meet the Workflow Builder
artifactLines: npm install -g n8n → n8n start → open localhost:5678.
Node = one unit of work. Connection = data path. Execute = run the sequence.
Error? Read the message — it names what is missing.
n8n.io/docs is authoritative for your version; UI changes between releases.
Before E3: confirm your API key is ready — you will need it when wiring the API node.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The deliverable for this episode is simple: n8n running on your machine with a blank canvas open. Here is the prompt that starts it — run it in a terminal, not in Claude. Step 1: npm install -g n8n. Step 2: n8n start. Step 3: open a browser to http://localhost:5678. If the canvas loads, you are done with Episode 2. If something fails, paste this prompt into Claude: n8n is not starting on my machine. I ran npm install -g n8n and then n8n start. The terminal shows this error: [paste the exact error text here]. What is the most likely cause and how do I fix it? That prompt gives Claude the exact context it needs. A vague 'n8n isn't working' prompt gets a vague answer. Paste the exact error text and Claude will usually identify the fix in one response.

props.segment: Install n8n and Meet the Workflow Builder

beats.narration_text: Install n8n and Meet the Workflow Builder. Liam, in for Bear.
